title: Hildegard of Bingen: An Interview with Dr. Margot Fassler – WAM! Women And Music
excerpts:
Section Title: Hildegard of Bingen: An Interview with Dr. Margot Fassler > ... > Transcript of the interview:
Content:
**Narrator:** Hildegard of Bingen was an extraordinary person. She travelled extensively, produced an enormous body of work, and exerted significant power and influence in the Catholic church at a time when women seldom had a voice.
**Fassler:** The first question I’m going to talk about is the understanding of Hildegard’s works and what was her most significant contribution, whether to knowledge or to art. And I would say with Hildegard that’s not a particularly great question, because she’s a theologian who is also a composer, and an artist and dramatist. And so it’s very difficult to extricate one thing from another. Her work as a theologian, her massive treatises that she wrote—three of them in number—and then her work as an artist are completely, holistically bound to one another.

title: Hildegard of Bingen: The Nun Who Loved the Earth - Theosophical Society in America
excerpts:
Section Title: Hildegard of Bingen: The Nun Who Loved the Earth
Content:
**Printed in the Spring 2017** **issue of Quest magazine.
** **Citation:** **Overweg, Cynthia** **, "Hildegard of Bingen: The Nun Who Loved the Earth" Quest 105:3(Summer 2017) pg. 21 -25**
**By Cynthia Overweg**
The earth sustains humanity. It must not be injured; it must not be destroyed.
—Hildegard of Bingen
Hildegard of Bingen was no ordinary nun. The beloved Benedictine abbess stood at the epicenter of medieval Europe as a visionary and mystic. Famous for her visions of celestial wonders and vivid descriptions of an ordered and divine universe, she was a spiritual beacon to thousands of people who flocked to her monastery, seeking her advice and counsel.

title: Saint Hildegard of Bingen — My Catholic Life!
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > **September 17: Saint Hildegard of Bingen, Virgin and Doctor of the Church—Optional Memorial**
Content:
In 1142, Abbess Hildegard intensely sensed God commanding her to write her visions and share her divinely infused knowledge. She spent the rest of her life transcribing all that she understood by this infusion of the living light. Her first work, completed in 1151, described twenty-six visions with her commentary, known as *Scivias* —“Know the Ways.” These visions and commentaries touch on every aspect of faith, from the creation of the world to the end of time.

title: Hildegard of Bingen
excerpts: **Hildegard of Bingen** <sup>[[ a ]]()</sup> OSB ( German : *Hildegard von Bingen* , pronounced [[ˈhɪldəɡaʁt fɔn ˈbɪŋən]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") ; Latin : *Hildegardis Bingensis* ; c. 1098 – 17 September 1179), also known as the **Sibyl of the Rhine** , was a German Benedictine abbess and polymath active as a writer, composer, philosopher, mystic , visionary , and as a medical writer and practitioner of the Catholic Church during the High Middle Ages . <sup>[[ 1 ]](,_Judith_M_2001-2)</sup> <sup>[[ 2 ]]()</sup> She is one of the best-known composers of sacred monophony , as well as the most recorded in modern history. <sup>[[ 3 ]]()</sup> She has been considered by a number of scholars to be the founder of scientific natural history in Germany. <sup>[[ 4 ]]()</sup>

title: Saint Hildegard von Bingen – A remarkable woman for our times
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Hildegard von Bingen: A Monumental Theological Figure of the Middle Ages
Content:
What was Hildegard von Bingen theology? As a [Benedictine nun](https://www.bingen.de/hildegard/spurensuche-hildegard/abtei-st-hildegard-eibingen) , St. Hildegard von Bingen accomplished an impressive amount during her lifetime. Born in 1098 in Bermersheim vor der Höhe, Germany, she went on to found two women’s monastic communities and create a wealth of theological and visionary works. In addition to her written works, Hildegard also [designed illustrations, interpreted the Bible, and composed liturgical songs](https://www.bingen.de/hildegard/museum-am-strom-hildegard) . She was also a pioneer in the field of natural science and medicine, developing important works on these subjects.

title: Saint Hildegard von Bingen (1098-1179): "the light of her people and of her time" | The History of Dermatology Society
excerpts:
Section Title: Saint Hildegard von Bingen (1098-1179): “the light of her people and of her time”
Content:
**Introduction**
The first time I ever heard the name of Hildegard von Bingen was when I was preparing a paper about Giovan Bonomo, the discoverer of the etiology of scabies, for the 1997 History of Dermatology symposium. In the twelfth century, Hildegard had already commented about scabies. The 1998 symposium which subject was ‘Women in Dermatology’ gave me the great opportunity of researching about life in the Middle Ages and about the life and work of Hildegard von Bingen. By reading about her I learned to deeply respect her: she was one of the most fascinating and remarkable personalities of her time. I was astonished to find out how extremely creative she was, and how her creations and observations related to so many different sectors and areas of Medieval life, including Medicine, still influence modern times and are so up-to-date.
